lesson_title: Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.front: What is the purpose of sorting an array?
cards.back:
Arranging all elements in a particular order, which makes:
  • Searching more efficient
  • Finding extremes easier (min/max at start/end)
  • Data presentation more organized
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.text: To swap two values in C++, use {{c1::std::swap(x, y)}} from the {{c2::<utility>}} header.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.front: What are the main steps of selection sort?
cards.back:
• Starting at index 0, find the smallest element in the entire array
  • Swap it with the element at index 0
  • Repeat for subsequent indices until done
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.text: In selection sort, the outer loop can stop at {{c1::length - 1}} instead of length because the last element will already be {{c2::sorted}} by that point.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.question: What does this selection sort code fragment do?
cards.answer: Finds the index of the smallest element in the remaining unsorted portion of the array.
cards.explanation: It starts by assuming startIndex is the smallest, then checks all subsequent elements. If a smaller element is found, it updates smallestIndex.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.text: To sort an array using the standard library, call {{c1::std::sort(std::begin(array), std::end(array))}} from the {{c2::<algorithm>}} header.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.front: What order does std::sort use by default?
cards.back: Ascending order, using operator< to compare pairs of elements.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.concept: When sorting is worth the cost
cards.explanation:
Sorting is expensive, but can enable:
  • Binary search (1M elements searchable with only 20 comparisons)
  • Immediate access to min/max values
  • Early termination when searching alphabetically
Only worth it if you'll search the array many times.
examples: // Unsorted: must check all elements for max
// Sorted: max is at array[0] or array[length-1]
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.text: Bubble sort works by comparing {{c1::adjacent pairs}} of elements and swapping them if needed, causing larger elements to "bubble" to the {{c2::end}} of the array.
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.question: How would you modify selection sort to sort in descending order?
cards.answer: Change < to > and rename smallestIndex to largestIndex
cards.explanation: Instead of finding the smallest element, find the largest element for each position: if (array[currentIndex] > array[largestIndex])
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.front: What are two common optimizations for bubble sort?
cards.back:
• Don't recheck elements that are already sorted at the end
  • If no swaps occur in an iteration, the array is sorted - terminate early
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort

cards.back: A loop inside another loop, commonly used in sorting algorithms (outer loop for position, inner loop to find element)
cards.source: 18.1 - Sorting an array using selection sort
